sentences.text: I was close to death, yes.
That was what the super wanted.
But it didn't work again because we found this doctor.
And he gave me something else, and it pulled me out.
Well, he wanted me dead.
Remember, he said, I-- I work with you.
Yet it didn't work, not one time.
The first time, he didn't want me to get the apartment, and so forth And he said, I'll-- I'll-- I'll get even with you.
And this is a man who lived on our money all his life because we lived there.
But that's how people are.
So anyhow, we-- I got better.
And the bombing really, really started.
December was incredible.
Constant, non-stop.
And what happened by then, the Russians also bombed, the Russians, the British, and the Americans.
But the Russian bomb never went all the way down, only the last floor, and so our floor, just our apartment was gone with the Russian bomb.
And all my books, everything fell down downstairs.
And my father risked his life to save all my books and all the pictures.
And that's why you see the scratches.
That's all bomb scratches on the pictures, if you find it.
And so I got well back home.
And even somehow a telegram arrived from my fiance from Sweden that I hope you're all right, and I am waiting for you, and we get married.
And I really wasn't in love with the man who saved my life, so what can you do?
But in any case, we were still there.
And we had to go down to the basement many, many times.
And I said, there were so many new faces there.
And there was one very, very good looking gentleman with a younger gentleman, what was his son, and his mother, and his wife.
And somehow, we took a liking to each other.
I don't know why.
And it turned out that he was a Hungarian general who ran away from the Hungarian army.
And his son already committed suicide.
But thank god, didn't die and didn't want to work with them.
And so they took another ID for them.
And they got an apartment in our house.
So we became friends.
And he was fascinated by Wallenberg and what he's doing.
And he was really my friend until-- we had-- still, we could hear some news in the radio.
But there was no connection.
In the meantime, they bombed all the bridges and all that.
So it was a very hard way to know what's happening.
But He had the BBC all the time on.
So one day, he tells me, Aggie, come.
There is something happening.
And we go to the big door.
We have a big door in Hungary.
And we saw the German weapons all lying down all the way on the floor.
I said, my God, the Russians must be coming.
And the Germans went to the Buda side, and the Russians came on the Pest side.
And that, too, how-- sometimes, I don't know why you don't die.
I usually did-- opposite us used to be the Minister of Culture.
And they had in the room there, a huge, big clock.
And I always looked at that clock from my side because I could see.
And I came home from the embassy, and I don't know why I decided, this time, I'll step off and go over there to see.
And as I stepped off and walked, half of our roof fell down.
Now, you see, that's what I'm always saying.
It's miracle why you are alive.
You could have been killed any time, any moment, all the time.
But this day, I had to step down.
For 20 years, I would see the clock from my side.
This time, I decide, no, I walked down.
And I wasn't killed.
And I went home after like this.
And I said, Mommy and Poppy, you don't believe what happened.
It was incredible.
And now, thanks to this general, we heard it on the BBC that Romania was already freed.
Free.
There were French, British, and Russians in there.
But it was free.
So we thought, OK, we'll see what's happening to all of us.
In the meantime, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I said, the bridges were down and so forth.
And you couldn't really get to much-- we moved in-- in the meantime, one of my aunt and uncle moved in there too.
So we all went there.
And in Budapest, they still had houses where there was water out in the middle yard in the middle.
And there, again, we don't know why we lived.
I went down, I wanted to bring some water home so that my father can shave.
He was such a wonderful man.
And they were still shooting from Buda to Pest.
But there, you couldn't see the bombs or anything.
All you could hear is [WHISTLING] and then wherever it stopped, somebody dies.
So I walked out.
And an old gentleman came down from the housing.
Wife was at the window.
And he said hello to me.
And we walked.
And suddenly we hear this [WHISTLING].
And the old gentleman falls dead on my side.
And, again, I was like that.
Why him?
Why not me?
Why?
And I was so surprised, you know?
And the poor wife up there, she was screaming.
And we had to get him up.
It was horrifying.
But these little things happened all the time.
Then, of course, the Soviets walked in.
And that was even worse because they came from Siberia all the way.
The Russian soldiers, believe it or not, came without shoes all the way form Siberia.
They never saw a bathroom in their life.
So they made their business in the bathtub and  they washed their face in the toilet.
The Russian officers, however, came in American Jeeps and dressed up.
And these horrible soldiers were constantly-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] excuse me.
They were constantly drunk.
And in the afternoon, our old people used to sleep.
So the general's son-- he was very much in love with my cousin who died in polio-- and myself, we went down to the basement.
But the basement had only one daughter-- one door.
And we never played cards before, but you had to do something.
And we played cards.
And I was sitting against the wall when two drunken Russian soldiers walked in and they demanded gold teeth, gold watch.
And my super was there.
And evidently, he spoke Russian.
I didn't know that.
And we told him that we don't have any gold anyway.
He must have said something else because-- again, the miracle-- one card fell down and the guy shot.
But by that time, I was down to pick up the card.
And it went into the wall.
But my cousin and my friend thought that I was shot.
And they took me up.
And then they heard it from all over, Aggie shot, Aggie was shot.
Again, the super didn't do the right way.
I didn't die, but why did I go down to pick up that card at the right moment?
Why?
It went right behind me.
So then they called the Russian officer.
And, of course, they took off, and they apologized.
But that was worse because the Russians, they picked up people from the street and took them to Siberia, never mind if it was a Christian, a Jew, or young or old.
They're gone.
So then there we were.
And my father said, now are you going to stay in Hungary?
Do you?
And I said, Papa, I'm only going to stay if I can get a good job.
But they probably won't give me one.
And there was an employment office, and we went there.
My father came with me.
And the first question was, were you a Nazi?
I said, no.
Are you a Communist?
I said, no.
What are you?
I said, democrat.
No-- no job.
And I was kicked out.
OK.
So I said, Papa, if I can, how-- maybe we have to leave from here.
And then the miracle happened.
On the boat-- they have to come over on a boat from Buda side because there was no bridges.
And over came a Czechoslovakian lawyer who was working with Raoul for a long time for Haggadish.
And he told me-- and he didn't tell me that Raoul wasn't with him.
He just said that the Swedish legation was taken by the Russian headquarters.
I says, oh my God.
He said and you have to help.
I said, yeah, what can I do?
He said, Romania is free.
We knew that already.
And there is still a Swedish legation.
You have to go there and speak to the ambassador.
And hopefully, they can bring them out.
I told my parents.
No, she can't go.
It's dangerous.
And I looked at my parents.
And I said, I know it's dangerous, Mommy and Poppy.
But they saved us all, 100,000 of us.
How can I say no?
Because what Raoul did at the last minute-- I forgot to tell you-- when he knew that the Russians are in there, one Hungarian who wanted him to help him came to him.
And he said, Raoul, I can just order to kill everybody in the ghetto.
We also had a ghetto.
And there was 70,000 people in there.
And on that day, it so happened two of  our Swedish Red Cross girls were in there delivering medication and letters.
And Raoul, without batting an eye, said go back to you headquarters.
Tell them, the Germans, to called off the killing.
And the guns were standing there.
So the Hungarian went.
So we were in the office, and Raoul looks at us.
And he says, now watch what I am going to do.
And he picked up the phone.
He spoke beautiful German.
And he says, this is Lieutenant Krausse.
I want to talk to General Schmidhuber.
[NON-ENGLISH] When lieutenant want's to speak, they give it to him.
And then he came to the phone.
Raoul said, I am Raoul Wallenberg, and I am very, very disappointed in you, General.
I understand you are a highly decorated, First World War officer.
How can you take it on your heart to kill 70,000 innocent people plus my two Swedish Red Cross girls?
Whereby the guy said, Eichmann ordered it.
Now, Eichmann could have stood right with him, right?
But our Raoul, typical, starts laughing.
He says, Eichmann, didn't you know Eichmann left Budapest?
I had lunch with him yesterday.
He never had.
Thereby, the general said, I didn't know.
He said, now, if you don't call off the killing within one minute, then you will be hanged.
And he hung up.
In a half a minute, he called off the killing, thereby saving 70,000 people.
All right.
And the 20,000 or 30,000 extra.
And then the man who made the-- the fake papers, Raoul met him, too.
And he was very angry with him that he made fake papers, because then ours won't be good.
And then typical, Raoul Wallenberg looked at him and said, but if you can save another human life, go ahead and do it.
So we probably have all of our fakes, too.
So in any case, this was the last minute.
And over comes the man, we have to help.
I said, well, how do I get there?
He said, the Romanians are allowed to repatriate.
So I'm getting you some false Romanian papers.
Just don't open your mouth.
So I said, yes.
And my parents were besides themselves.
I said, Mommy and Poppy, I will bring you all too.
But we have to try.
I mean, these people did everything for us.
So sure enough, we had to go on the same-- not first.
But first, we went on a normal train.
And we were told by some nice soldiers there to be very careful, because you'll never know when the Russians will pick you up and take you to Siberia or back to Hungary.
So good luck.
All I had was a knapsack.
The-- Mr Haggadish said, the Swedish paper should be in your behind.
And don't carry anything with you excepts these Romanian paper.
And don't open your mouth.
But it turned out that the other people were about like me, too.
They had-- not Romanian.
They were something else.
And it took us three days.
Now, the morning we already had to stop.
They said they stopped the train.
We can't go on.
And we went into a school, and we slept on the ground.
But when you're young, you sleep so well.
It doesn't Matter where, on your coat.
And then we went back, we had to go into a cattle car.
And then we got to the Romanian border.
A very nice soldier came.
And he said, now please, not one word out of you-- anyone of you.
No sneezing, no coughing because the Russians are all drunk.
But they come.
They will open the door because it is a cattle car.
Get under the-- what did we have-- straw-- and try to be quiet.
And they will go with the light.
And if-- So here we were.
That was our last chance.
I don't know how many we were in that one big place.
We all were very quiet.
And sure enough, they came singing.
And they opened the door and entered the light.
And then, boom's the door, and we moved.
And we all said, we are in Romania.
And we got in.
It's a small town, Arad.
And I had just one friend who came down and was in the music academy.
I knew her address.
I figured I'd go there.
And there was another friend of mine who was a baroness who was a great philanthropist.
And I was hoping that she's alive, the baroness from [NON-ENGLISH].
And I knew her address because she came to Hungary, and the two of us learned how to cook in the gas company.
And she became the philanthropist, and I became the great cook.
So that works out.
But she went home.
So we arrived to that little station, and nobody around, you know.
And it was rather dark, but very quiet.
And a young man came up to me.
He said, where are you going?
You never like to talk to anyone.
And I said, well, I have a friend.
I'm trying to go there.
Who are you?
He said, I'm a concert pianist.
So I put up my hand.
Ah, you are a concert pianist.
He said, yes.
And I came away from Hungary.
I escaped.
So I said, well, come with me.
I mean, all my friends can do is throw us both out.
And it must have been about 10 o'clock in the evening.
And there's a big, big courtyard by their house.
And there was a huge piano outside.
I said, Look, this is made for you.
There's a piano outside.
It was very funny.
And we knock at the door.
And my friend was screaming.
I mean, to see us suddenly.
And I was about 80 pounds, or not even that.
And they brought out food.
I said, look, we can't eat, but we throw up after like crazy.
Because we really didn't eat for weeks, for months, or something.
And then we had to go to the Red Cross to get shots.
But we already had all those shots, thank God.
And I had the papers.
But What was very funny, for months and months, I never had shoes on, only my ski boots.
So when I first put on the high heel shoes, I didn't know how to walk.
They had to hold me because I didn't know how to walk, you know?
But we went there.
And I wanted to go into every pastry shop to eat something.
But we never did.
And I am not a pastry eater.
And then I went to my friend.
And she was in a shop, too.
And then I told her that I had a pianist with me.
So she got excited, said, hey, we can make a concert, because people are so hungry for music.
So we had about three concerts in this tiny, little city of Arad.
And we earned enough money for me to buy silk stockings, and for him to-- whatever-- because we had nothing.
We shared that money.
And then I told Maricka that I have to go to Bucharest.
Now, what was your purpose now?
It was the end of the war.
Your parents were still in Hungary, you went to Romania.
What did you see your life being at this point?
How did you want to proceed?
I thought that saving the Swedish embassy from Budapest, that we will stay there for a while, and we can bring my parents with me, and we will all go together to Sweden.
And I will marry there, and so forth.
This was my dream.
No.
What happened is, first of all, my friend said, nobody can go to Bucharest, only on official business.
What is your business?
I said Maricka even for you, I can't say.
She said, I'll get you the papers.
And she did.
And in I go in the first class section.
And there is two Russian officers sitting.
And my stomach went, oops!
I didn't open my mouth.
And he started to talk to me in Russian.
And of course, I didn't answer.
And then, suddenly, he switched to English.
He said, you speak English?
I said, yes.
He said, who are you?
I said, I am a Romanian.
And he didn't speak Romanian, either.
And then they gave me the drinks, vodka and vodka.
And I threw it up, sort of.
I was scared stiff.
I said, they are going to attack me.
They were horrifying.
And then we came to Sinai.
And the train stopped, so I quickly ran off for a while.
And when I came back, the two officers weren't there.
Their coats were there, but they were not.
So the train starts moving.
And suddenly, I see them coming, looking around, frightened, closed the door, sit down.
And suddenly, the one turns to me and said, do you speak German?
And I said, yes.
He said I am a Jew.
Are you?
I said, yes.
Turned out they were dissidents, Russian dissidents.
One was a real Russia from Kiev, Christian.
And this one was a Jewish boy.
And they wanted to get to Israel.
And they had two-- so it was very interesting.
And then I told them what I had done.
So when I arrived there, they had connections with the Jewish whatever there.
So I had a room for the night.
So next day, I had to go to the Swedish embassy.
And a taxi took me there, and he said, are you afraid to go there to the Swedish embassy?
And he Spoke very nice German.
I said, no, why?
He said because the Russian headquarters is next door.
And then I can find out that this ambassador was so afraid because of these people.
But they wouldn't let me see him.
The secretary said, what do you want?
I said, I can't tell you.
So I sat there till 4 o'clock.
Then, she got tired of me.
And she said, OK, go and see the ambassador now.
That was an ambassador, not just a minister.
And I walked in and I told him who I was and why I came.
And the man screamed.
And why you only coming in now?
You were sitting outside?
I said, yes, sir.
So he pushed in the button.
And it turned out my fiance looking for me.
And I had the list they gave me about the other seven Swedes in Budapest.
And I had their name that they are all alive and they will come here.
Raoul Wallenberg not--
Raoul Wallen-- just the Swedish embassy.
Raoul Wallenberg, there was not a word.
But he knew that I worked with him.
And in eight days, they were in Bucharest, minus Raoul.
And then I looked at Ambassador [NON-ENGLISH] I mean, [NON-ENGLISH] was no ambassador then.
I said, where is Raoul?
He said, don't worry about him.
You know he will come.
They didn't know either.
So then the ambassador said, from now on, I want you only to carry a Swedish passport.
Don't talk any other language.
You don't speak any Romanian, and Hungarian, any German, any English.
You only speak Swedish.
So sure enough, this came very handy.
I knew three words in Swedish.
Thank you, which is [SWEDISH], brod, what is bread, and mjolk, what is milk.
And one day, sure enough, one of these officers stopped me on the street.
Paper?
He said it in every language.
And I just smiled.
So he took me into the police station, and I said, oh my god.
So all I could do is I put down the phone number.
And I called.
And comes-- what's his name?
[INAUDIBLE].
That was the name of the ambassador.
Frightened.
I could see that he was so frightened.
And as he walks in, I started to hit him with the three words over and over.
Mjolk, brod, [SWEDISH].
And I see that his face relaxed.
And of course, the Romanian didn't understand a word.
And they let me go with him.
And then we were almost at the car.
He said, how did you come to this idea?
Did you forgetting I worked with Wallenberg?
And that's what saved me, these three Swedish words.
So then, finally, they decided-- the Russians-- that we can leave with the 12 of them through Russia, and we can go to Sweden, the eight of us.
There was eight Swedes and these 12.
And we were ail happy we were out in [INAUDIBLE], you know, where they used to have the big bombing at the railway station.
And first the Swedes went on.
And then the Russians suddenly said, nyet, and they wouldn't let  us go with them.
And we got stuck in Bucharest.
What was bad-- what I did that time, I could-- through the Red Cross, I could send letters to my parents.
And I was-- what did I do-- writing for a newspaper there.
And I was eating frogs all the time because that-- I didn't have much money.
And they taught me how to take them out of the lake, and how to burn them.
And I had a very nice little room by a  lovely lady-- [CAR HORN] oh, please stop it-- lady.
And so we told them that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this is-- this is all my story, and it's wonderful here.
But when can we get away from here?
So my friend Maricka, from the little city.
She came every Friday.
There was in Romania-- I don't know how it is now.
Like we have the ice cream parlors, they have the caviar parlors.
And you can choose your caviar.
And then you go down in the bottom, and they have wonderful wooden tables, scrubbed.
And they bring you a glass of champagne, and toast, and your caviar.
And one day, while I was waiting for her, I went in to buy some handkerchiefs and the papers, and then I hear somebody talking French.
And I turn around, and this is my French underground friend.
And we're screaming, the two of us.
Then, it turned out I told him the story.
The other one already went home.
The Jewish boy went back, but he was still there.
But they were sent to Hungary to find out what's happening, and what the Germans are doing, and so forth.
So they were spies for them.
Wonderful, wonderful.
He was a pleasant--
And then all eight of us had to stay there until, suddenly, finally, we got a phone call again that we can leave on a South American-- not South American-- South African military plane.
And these two Russian dissidents, the one wrote a marvelous book, and he wanted me to take it.
But we were told to be careful because the Russian will make a-- and I can be arrested immediately.
So I couldn't take the book.
And I'm so sorry because the Russians did absolutely nothing.
We just walked out, the eight of us, into the South African plane.
And we were supposed to go to Bari.
Bari was the-- in Italy, the place for all the Allied military.
Did you know that?
That the Allied military was there?
And the British was really the head of it.
And it took us a few hours.
And it was a beautiful, beautiful trip, because you went through the Adriatic there.
And it was so interesting.
And we were-- it was a fighter plane, so where the guns were in before, that's where the seats were.
And there was only one more man in there, a big, fat gentleman from Brit-- you know, British.
And one of these ladies who had this Swedish passport, she was German.
And she couldn't speak Swedish.
And the two of us spoke German, but we didn't know what's going to happen to us because we were promised, when we get to Bari, the Swedes had a hotel for us.
And then from there we will go on.
So we arrived to Bari, and the British gave us a marvelous welcome.
And it was very exciting.
Freedom, freedom, we are here!
And there was just those real little sandwiches, and it was wonderful.
And then they put us in a truck to take us where we're supposed to go.
And only I opened my mouth, and I said this doesn't look like we are going into Bari to a hotel.
Oh, Aggie, of course.
That's your fantasy that we're not going.
Well, we didn't We ended up in a transit camp, what was actually a prison, all eight of us.
And in the prison now-- this was before for Jews.
And now the Germans were there, and the British were taking care of them.
It was a prison for them and also people who were waiting to go home, either to Italy, to wherever.
They were there.
But we were separated form them.
And we wanted to see a British officer.
And we went into huge big room for about 60 beds up and down, you know?
And I wanted to see a British officer.
And there wasn't any.
And there was a big hole there.
And I wanted to get out.
And this little British Cockney stands there with a gun.
He said, I shoot you if you come out.
So I had to start laughing, and I pulled my leg.
And I said, this is funny.
The Germans didn't kill me.
The Russians didn't kill me.
But my own allies is going to kill me because I go out.
So it was a weekend.
So there was a big, big bathroom that was for the horse before, but at least there was cold water, and we could wash.
And in the morning, we were interrogated.
Now, it turned out why we had to be there.
Because this British man was from the Scotland Yard.
And because we spoke German, the two of us with this old lady, he was sure that we are two Nazis running away.
That we had nothing to do with Germans.
So by the time we got to Bari, he said you better put them in there first.
So we were a whole month.
It's a long story how we were there for a month.
And in the meantime, we were interrogated also.
OK.
